reasoning: Total homicides in Trinidad and Tobago up to July 17, 2025 were 214, averaging approximately 33 total homicides per month. For this question to resolve positively, ACLED would need to record more than 204 fatalities in a 30-day window (i.e., a tenfold increase from the baseline of ~20/month). ACLED only captures a subset of total homicides (those related to political or organized armed violence). Achieving >204 ACLED fatalities in 30 days would require an unprecedented explosion of violence akin to an active civil war, representing a far higher rate than the country's worst crime waves. Furthermore, the Trinidad and Tobago government enacted a new State of Emergency in mid-July 2025 to heavily suppress gangs, relocate gang leaders from maximum-security prisons, and initiate broad military/police operations. This is likely to keep organized violence contained or at least well below 200+ fatalities per month. As such, a 10x spike is extremely unlikely across all time horizons. Probabilities are strictly bounded and slightly increase over time to reflect horizon uncertainty.
